name: Band Pull-Apart
resistance_profile: Ascending band tension in the transverse plane — resistance peaks at full horizontal abduction (arms spread wide), so it is hardest at peak contraction.
cues: Lead with the pinkies and let the thumbs rotate down.
Keep the hands at chest height — don't let them drift up into a shrug.
Stretch the band across your chest, not up toward your neck.
execution_steps: Hold a light resistance band with both hands at shoulder width, arms extended straight in front at chest height.
Set a small, fixed bend in the elbows and brace the torso so you don't lean back.
Pull the band apart by driving the hands out and back, leading with the pinkies, until the arms are roughly in line with the torso.
Squeeze the rear delts and draw the shoulder blades together for a brief hold at the fully-spread position.
Return under control to the start, resisting the band the whole way, and keep tension throughout.
good_when: You need a joint-friendly rear-delt and upper-back movement for warm-ups or high-rep finishers.
You train at home or while travelling and want a portable staple.
You want to reinforce scapular retraction and posture between heavier pulls.
bad_when: You need heavy, progressively overloaded rear-delt work — band tension caps out quickly.
The only band available is so strong that you shrug or lean to move it.
common_errors: Bending and re-extending the elbows to cheat the band apart.
Shrugging the traps to finish the rep.
Leaning the torso back to add momentum.
progressions: Use a thicker or shorter band, or shorten your grip, for more tension.
Add a 2-second hold at the fully-apart position.
regressions: Use a lighter band or a wider grip.
Perform it seated to remove any torso momentum.
